{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its annual festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | Name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | Image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | Location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | Festival Deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine Rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | Established = Meiji 37 (1904) | Main Hall = | Alternate Names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | Annual Festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | Ceremonies = | Map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting Festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its annual festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | Name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | Image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | Location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | Festival Deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine Rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | Established = Meiji 37 (1904) | Main Hall = | Alternate Names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | Annual Festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | Ceremonies = | Map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting Festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its annual festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | Name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | Image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | Location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | Coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | Festival Deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine Rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | Established = Meiji 37 (1904) | Main Hall = | Alternate Names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | Annual Festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | Ceremonies = | Map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting Festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | festival deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | annual festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:dd]] [[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:dd]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:dd]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia29]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}

The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Pgoodbye $129]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Pgoodbye $129]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}}
