revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]]}} [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{citation needed|Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}}

Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine Rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]]

Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:qq]]{{citation needed|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]]

revision.comment: 55 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宮城縣護國神社 up to rev 101150390
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:05 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 宮城縣護國神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[青葉区 (仙台市)|青葉区]]川内1 |緯度度 = 38 |緯度分 = 15 |緯度秒 = 9 |経度度 = 140 |経度分 = 51 |経度秒 = 20 |ISO = JP-04 |祭神 = 国事殉難者 |社格 = 内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 |創建 = 明治37年（1904年） |本殿 = |別名 = [[招魂社]]（創建～1939年）
宮城神社（[[連合国軍占領下の日本|連合国軍占領期]]） |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月30日]]・[[5月1日]]・[[10月23日]] |神事 = |地図 = Sendai#Japan Miyagi |ラベル位置 = left }} [[ファイル:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|正面大鳥居]] [[ファイル:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|拝殿と鳥居の遠景]] '''宮城縣護國神社'''（みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[青葉区 (仙台市)|青葉区]]川内にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。[[青葉山 (宮城県)|青葉山]]の[[仙台城]]（青葉城）本丸跡に創建された。[[明治維新]]以降の諸事変、戦役における[[宮城県]]関係あるいは縁故のある[[戦死]]・殉難者、5万6千余[[柱]]を祀る。神紋は十五菊に桜。 == 歴史 == [[日清戦争]]後の[[明治]]31年（[[1898年]]）に組織された昭忠会を母体として、[[日露戦争]]中の明治37年（[[1904年]]）[[8月27日]]、現在地に「'''[[招魂社]]'''」を建立し、[[大日本帝国陸軍]]・[[第2師団 (日本軍)|第二師団]]管下（[[第2師管]]：[[宮城県]]・[[福島県]]・[[新潟県]]）および[[山形県]]の戦死者の一部を祀ったのを起源とする。[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）の[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]令により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定[[護国神社]]となり、「'''宮城縣護國神社'''」に改称した。 昭和20年（[[1945年]]）[[7月10日]]の[[仙台空襲]]で社殿を焼失し、神体は市内の[[秋保神社]]・仮宮に遷された。[[戦後]]の[[連合国軍占領下の日本|連合国軍占領期]]には、仙台城二の丸を中心に進駐した[[米軍]]に隣接し、「'''宮城神社'''」と称していた。昭和25年（[[1950年]]）[[国有地]]払い下げにより当社が本丸一帯を所有すると、[[仙台市]]は仙台城本丸の[[私有財産]]化に反対し、土地の一部を買い上げて[[公有財産]]化したが、当社所有地は残った。 進駐軍が去ると昭和32年（[[1957年]]）に社名を「'''宮城縣護國神社'''」に戻し、昭和33年（[[1958年]]）に[[伊勢神宮]]別宮「[[風宮]]」の旧社殿を移築して復興した。 昭和38年（[[1963年]]）[[5月18日]]、[[昭和天皇]]、[[香淳皇后]]が第14回[[全国植樹祭]]に合わせて県内を行幸啓。護国神社が訪問先の一つとなった{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=124 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}。 昭和52年（[[1976年]]）[[10月12日]]、正面拝殿に[[火炎瓶]]が投げ込まれ炎上。神社関係者が消火にあたり、鉄製の拝殿の扉を焦がしたところで鎮火させた。「天皇陛下御在位五十年記念事業」に反対する[[過激派]]の犯行と見られている護国神社に火炎びん 仙台『朝日新聞』1976年（昭和51年）10月13日朝刊、13版、23面。 [[平成]]19年（[[2007年]]）10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 なお、第二師団の将兵・軍属の墓は、仙台市青葉区小松島の[[常盤台墓苑]]（旧・陸軍墓地）にある[https://www.pref.miyagi.jp/soshiki/syahuku/shisetsu.html 常盤台霊苑・宮城之塔の紹介]（宮城県）{{PDFlink|[http://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/BO/0082/BO00820L019.pdf 「万骨枯る」空間の形成 －陸軍墓地の制度と実態を中心に－]}}（[[佛教大学]]）。 == 境内社 == 別宮として「浦安宮」があり、右宮には[[天照大神|天照坐皇御神]]ほか三柱、左宮には当地に古くから鎮座する白水稲荷大神と[[仙台藩]]藩祖の[[伊達政宗]]を祀る。 == 福娘 == 例年、[[年末年始]]を中心に参拝客をもてなす「福娘」（青葉城ミス福娘）が[[12月]][[上旬]]に選ばれる。内訳は、ミス福娘1名、準ミス福娘2名、福娘5～7名。 「ミス福娘」出身の有名人として[[白鳥百合子]]がいるほか、在任中に[[ミス日本]]に選出された者もいる[http://gokokujinja.org/gokoku1/fuku/fuku.htm 青葉城ミス「福娘」募集!]（宮城縣護國神社）。 == 仙台藩との関係 == {{節スタブ}} 当社は[[仙台城]]址に鎮座しているが、仙台城は[[戊辰戦争]]で[[官軍]]に占領されてから[[第二次世界大戦]]後の米軍の仙台進駐まで、[[政府]]側の軍事拠点が置かれていた所である。そのため当社は、[[靖国神社]]と同様に「[[天皇]]・[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]・政府側の立場で命を捧げた者」を[[祭神]]としており、戊辰戦争に敗戦して[[賊軍]]となった[[仙台藩]]関係者は祀られていない。戦没仙台藩士1260人は、[[伊達政宗]]の[[霊廟]]「[[瑞鳳殿]]」に明治10年（[[1877年]]）にたてられた弔魂碑（仙台市所有）に祀られている。また、政宗を祀る[[青葉神社]]も、明治7年（[[1874年]]）に[[北山五山]]・[[東昌寺 (仙台市)|東昌寺]]敷地内に創建された。このような背景があるため、当社が戦後も仙台城址を所有し、仙台城址観光の収益を[[独占]]する体制を批判する意見が聞かれることがある。その一方で、仙台市が所有する区画には現在、観光客向けの土産物屋・飲食店の類はないため、仙台城址観光客の満足度を高める一助となっている側面もある。 当社が仙台城址にあるため、「[[戦国BASARA]]」ファン（[[歴女]]）が政宗を祀る神社と勘違いして参拝するようになり、ゲームのキャラクター化された政宗らを描いた[[絵馬]]を奉納する例が目立つようになった[https://web.archive.org/web/20090517014629/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/lifestyle/090514/sty0905142302009-n1.htm 萌え系絵馬に英霊戸惑う!?　宮城県護国神社「成り立ち理解を」]（[[産経新聞]] 2009年5月14日）。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[[靖国神社]] *[[国家神道]] *[[青葉山公園]] == 外部リンク == * [http://gokokujinja.org 宮城縣護國神社]（公式[[ウェブサイト]]。[[BGM]]あり） * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 宮城縣護國神社]（宮城県[[神社庁]]） {{DEFAULTSORT:みやきけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:仙台市の神社]] [[Category:仙台市青葉区の建築物]] [[Category:仙台市青葉区の歴史]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:別表神社]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:05 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 宮城縣護國神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[青葉区 (仙台市)|青葉区]]川内1 |緯度度 = 38 |緯度分 = 15 |緯度秒 = 9 |経度度 = 140 |経度分 = 51 |経度秒 = 20 |ISO = JP-04 |祭神 = 国事殉難者 |社格 = 内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社 |創建 = 明治37年（1904年） |本殿 = |別名 = [[招魂社]]（創建～1939年）
宮城神社（[[連合国軍占領下の日本|連合国軍占領期]]） |札所等 = |例祭 = [[4月30日]]・[[5月1日]]・[[10月23日]] |神事 = |地図 = Sendai#Japan Miyagi |ラベル位置 = left }} [[ファイル:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|正面大鳥居]] [[ファイル:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|拝殿と鳥居の遠景]] '''宮城縣護國神社'''（みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[青葉区 (仙台市)|青葉区]]川内にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。[[青葉山 (宮城県)|青葉山]]の[[仙台城]]（青葉城）本丸跡に創建された。[[明治維新]]以降の諸事変、戦役における[[宮城県]]関係あるいは縁故のある[[戦死]]・殉難者、5万6千余[[柱]]を祀る。神紋は十五菊に桜。 == 歴史 == [[日清戦争]]後の[[明治]]31年（[[1898年]]）に組織された昭忠会を母体として、[[日露戦争]]中の明治37年（[[1904年]]）[[8月27日]]、現在地に「'''[[招魂社]]'''」を建立し、[[大日本帝国陸軍]]・[[第2師団 (日本軍)|第二師団]]管下（[[第2師管]]：[[宮城県]]・[[福島県]]・[[新潟県]]）および[[山形県]]の戦死者の一部を祀ったのを起源とする。[[昭和]]14年（[[1939年]]）の[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]令により[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]指定[[護国神社]]となり、「'''宮城縣護國神社'''」に改称した。 昭和20年（[[1945年]]）[[7月10日]]の[[仙台空襲]]で社殿を焼失し、神体は市内の[[秋保神社]]・仮宮に遷された。[[戦後]]の[[連合国軍占領下の日本|連合国軍占領期]]には、仙台城二の丸を中心に進駐した[[米軍]]に隣接し、「'''宮城神社'''」と称していた。昭和25年（[[1950年]]）[[国有地]]払い下げにより当社が本丸一帯を所有すると、[[仙台市]]は仙台城本丸の[[私有財産]]化に反対し、土地の一部を買い上げて[[公有財産]]化したが、当社所有地は残った。 進駐軍が去ると昭和32年（[[1957年]]）に社名を「'''宮城縣護國神社'''」に戻し、昭和33年（[[1958年]]）に[[伊勢神宮]]別宮「[[風宮]]」の旧社殿を移築して復興した。 昭和38年（[[1963年]]）[[5月18日]]、[[昭和天皇]]、[[香淳皇后]]が第14回[[全国植樹祭]]に合わせて県内を行幸啓。護国神社が訪問先の一つとなった{{Cite book |和書 |author=原武史 |title=昭和天皇御召列車全記録 |publisher=新潮社 |year=2016-09-30 |page=124 |isbn=978-4-10-320523-4}}。 昭和52年（[[1976年]]）[[10月12日]]、正面拝殿に[[火炎瓶]]が投げ込まれ炎上。神社関係者が消火にあたり、鉄製の拝殿の扉を焦がしたところで鎮火させた。「天皇陛下御在位五十年記念事業」に反対する[[過激派]]の犯行と見られている護国神社に火炎びん 仙台『朝日新聞』1976年（昭和51年）10月13日朝刊、13版、23面。 [[平成]]19年（[[2007年]]）10月には[[特攻勇士の像]]が建立された。 なお、第二師団の将兵・軍属の墓は、仙台市青葉区小松島の[[常盤台墓苑]]（旧・陸軍墓地）にある[https://www.pref.miyagi.jp/soshiki/syahuku/shisetsu.html 常盤台霊苑・宮城之塔の紹介]（宮城県）{{PDFlink|[http://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/BO/0082/BO00820L019.pdf 「万骨枯る」空間の形成 －陸軍墓地の制度と実態を中心に－]}}（[[佛教大学]]）。 == 境内社 == 別宮として「浦安宮」があり、右宮には[[天照大神|天照坐皇御神]]ほか三柱、左宮には当地に古くから鎮座する白水稲荷大神と[[仙台藩]]藩祖の[[伊達政宗]]を祀る。 == 福娘 == 例年、[[年末年始]]を中心に参拝客をもてなす「福娘」（青葉城ミス福娘）が[[12月]][[上旬]]に選ばれる。内訳は、ミス福娘1名、準ミス福娘2名、福娘5～7名。 「ミス福娘」出身の有名人として[[白鳥百合子]]がいるほか、在任中に[[ミス日本]]に選出された者もいる[http://gokokujinja.org/gokoku1/fuku/fuku.htm 青葉城ミス「福娘」募集!]（宮城縣護國神社）。 == 仙台藩との関係 == {{節スタブ}} 当社は[[仙台城]]址に鎮座しているが、仙台城は[[戊辰戦争]]で[[官軍]]に占領されてから[[第二次世界大戦]]後の米軍の仙台進駐まで、[[政府]]側の軍事拠点が置かれていた所である。そのため当社は、[[靖国神社]]と同様に「[[天皇]]・[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]・政府側の立場で命を捧げた者」を[[祭神]]としており、戊辰戦争に敗戦して[[賊軍]]となった[[仙台藩]]関係者は祀られていない。戦没仙台藩士1260人は、[[伊達政宗]]の[[霊廟]]「[[瑞鳳殿]]」に明治10年（[[1877年]]）にたてられた弔魂碑（仙台市所有）に祀られている。また、政宗を祀る[[青葉神社]]も、明治7年（[[1874年]]）に[[北山五山]]・[[東昌寺 (仙台市)|東昌寺]]敷地内に創建された。このような背景があるため、当社が戦後も仙台城址を所有し、仙台城址観光の収益を[[独占]]する体制を批判する意見が聞かれることがある。その一方で、仙台市が所有する区画には現在、観光客向けの土産物屋・飲食店の類はないため、仙台城址観光客の満足度を高める一助となっている側面もある。 当社が仙台城址にあるため、「[[戦国BASARA]]」ファン（[[歴女]]）が政宗を祀る神社と勘違いして参拝するようになり、ゲームのキャラクター化された政宗らを描いた[[絵馬]]を奉納する例が目立つようになった[https://web.archive.org/web/20090517014629/http://sankei.jp.msn.com/life/lifestyle/090514/sty0905142302009-n1.htm 萌え系絵馬に英霊戸惑う!?　宮城県護国神社「成り立ち理解を」]（[[産経新聞]] 2009年5月14日）。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} *[[靖国神社]] *[[国家神道]] *[[青葉山公園]] == 外部リンク == * [http://gokokujinja.org 宮城縣護國神社]（公式[[ウェブサイト]]。[[BGM]]あり） * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 宮城縣護國神社]（宮城県[[神社庁]]） {{DEFAULTSORT:みやきけんここくしんしや}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine Rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:History of Aoba-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Expanded pages from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 7]] [[Category:qq]]{{citation needed|Miyagi Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] {{translated page|ja|宮城縣護國神社|version=101150390|comment=Imported full ja history}}

It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} ==Overview== {{Cite sources|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ }} {{translated page|ja|宮城縣護國神社|version=101150390|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]]

The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ }} {{translated page|ja|宮城縣護國神社|version=101150390|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo Shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine Rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} ==Overview== {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree-Planting Festival|National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ }} {{translated page|ja|宮城縣護國神社|version=101150390|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]]

Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ }} {{translated page|ja|宮城縣護國神社|version=101150390|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:仙台市青葉区の建築物]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Sendai, Miyagi prefecture, Japan}} {{unreferenced|date=December 2021}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Gokoku Shrine | native_name = 宮城縣護國神社 | native_name_lang = ja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG | location = 1 Kawauchi, [[Aoba-ku, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi]] 980-0862 | deity = Martyrs of the State | festivals = April 30, May 1, October 23 | country = {{flag|Japan}} | type = [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku]], [[Beppyo Shrine|beppyo]] | established = {{start date and age|1904}} |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Hall of Worship 2010-06-03.JPG|250px]]
Worship Hall | location = Kawauchi 1, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|38|15|9|N|140|51|20|E|region:JP-04|display=inline,title}} | deity = Spirits of those who died in national service | Shrine Rank = Shrine designated by the Minister of Home Affairs • Beppyo Shrine | established = Meiji 37 (1904) | main hall = | alternate names = [[Shokonsha]] (from its founding until 1939)
Miyagi Shrine (during the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]]) | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[April 30]], [[May 1]], [[October 23]] | ceremony = | map = Sendai#Japan Miyagi | Label Position = left }} '''Miyagiken Gokoku Shrine''' (宮城縣護國神社, ''Miyagiken gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]], Japan. It enshrines the [[kami]] of "martyrs of the state" (国事殉難者) and its festivals take place on April 30, May 1, and October 23. It was established in 1904 and originally referred to as ''Shōkonsha'' (招魂社). Its current name dates to 1939. {{Commons category|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} ==Overview== {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017 (Monday) 09:05 (UTC)}} [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Gate 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Main Torii]] [[File:Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Overall View 2010-06-03.JPG|250px|right|thumb|Distant view of the worship hall and torii]] '''Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (みやぎけんごこくじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] (Gokoku Shrine) located in Kawauchi, [[Aoba Ward, Sendai|Aoba Ward]], [[Sendai]], [[Miyagi Prefecture]]. It was founded on the site of the main enclosure of [[Sendai Castle]] (Aoba Castle) on [[Mount Aoba (Miyagi Prefecture)|Mount Aoba]]. The shrine enshrines over 56,000 spirits of those from Miyagi Prefecture or related to the area who died in various conflicts and wars since the [[Meiji Restoration]]. The shrine crest is a combination of a 15-petal chrysanthemum and cherry blossoms. == History == The shrine originated from the Shouchu Society, organized in Meiji 31 (1898) after the [[First Sino-Japanese War]]. During the [[Russo-Japanese War]], on August 27, Meiji 37 (1904), a "Shokonsha" (招魂社) was established at the current site to enshrine some of the war dead from Miyagi Prefecture, Fukushima Prefecture, Niigata Prefecture, and Yamagata Prefecture under the jurisdiction of the [[2nd Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|Second Division]]. In Showa 14 (1939), it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine." On July 10, Showa 20 (1945), the shrine buildings were destroyed in the [[Sendai air raid]], and the deity was temporarily moved to [[Akiu Shrine]] in the city. During the [[Allied Occupation of Japan]], the shrine was located adjacent to the US Army's base centered on the secondary enclosure of Sendai Castle and was called "Miyagi Shrine." In Showa 25 (1950), following the transfer of state-owned land, the shrine came to own the entire main enclosure of Sendai Castle. The city of Sendai opposed the privatization of the historic site and purchased part of the land to make it public property, but the shrine retained ownership of some areas. After the departure of the occupation forces, the shrine reverted to its name "Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine" in Showa 32 (1957) and was rebuilt in Showa 33 (1958) using the old shrine buildings of the detached shrine "Kazenomiyagu" of [[Ise Grand Shrine]]. On May 18, Showa 38 (1963), Emperor [[Showa Emperor|Showa]] and Empress [[Kojun Empress|Kojun]] visited the shrine during their tour of the prefecture for the 14th [[National Tree-Planting Festival|National Tree Planting festival]]. On October 12, Showa 52 (1976), a Molotov cocktail was thrown into the front worship hall, causing a fire. Shrine personnel extinguished the fire, which scorched the iron doors of the worship hall. The attack was believed to be the work of radicals opposing the "50th Anniversary of the Emperor's Reign Commemorative Project." In Heisei 19 (2007), a statue honoring kamikaze warriors was erected. The graves of the soldiers and military personnel of the Second Division are located in the [[Tokiwadai Cemetery]] (formerly the Army Cemetery) in Komatsushima, Aoba Ward, Sendai. == Sub-Shrines == The shrine includes the "Urakasu-no-Miya," where the right shrine enshrines the [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Omikami]] and other deities, and the left shrine enshrines the Shirazu Inari Okami and [[Date Masamune]], the founder of the [[Sendai Domain]]. == Fukumusume == Each year, "Fukumusume" (lucky maidens) are selected in early December to welcome worshippers, primarily during the year-end and New Year periods. The selection includes one Miss Fukumusume, two runner-up Miss Fukumusume, and five to seven Fukumusume. Notable former Fukumusume include [[Yuriko Shiratori]], and some have been selected as [[Miss Nippon]] during their tenure. == Relationship with the Sendai Domain == {{Section stub}} The shrine is located on the site of [[Sendai Castle]], which, after the [[Boshin War]], became a military base for government forces until the post-[[World War II]] occupation by US forces. Therefore, like [[Yasukuni Shrine]], it enshrines those who gave their lives for the emperor, the court, and the government, and does not enshrine those from the defeated Sendai Domain in the Boshin War. The 1,260 Sendai Domain warriors who died in the war are commemorated by a monument erected in Meiji 10 (1877) at the mausoleum of [[Date Masamune]] (Zuihoden), which is owned by the city of Sendai. Additionally, [[Aoba Shrine]], which enshrines Masamune, was established in Meiji 7 (1874) on the grounds of [[Tosho-ji]] in [[Kitayama Gozan]], Sendai. Due to this historical background, there are criticisms that the shrine's ownership of the Sendai Castle site and monopoly over the tourism revenue post-war is unfair. However, since the Sendai City-owned section does not have souvenir shops or restaurants, the shrine's activities contribute to visitor satisfaction. Because the shrine is located at the Sendai Castle site, fans of the game "Sengoku BASARA" often mistakenly visit the shrine believing it enshrines Date Masamune, leaving ema (votive tablets) featuring characters from the game. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat|Miyagiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [[Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[State Shinto]] * [[Aobayama Park]] == External Links == * [http://gokokujinja.org Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (official website, with background music) * [http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010043 Miyagi Prefecture Gokoku Shrine] (Miyagi Prefecture Shrine Office) {{Shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] ==External links== *[https://gokokujinja.org Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|38.25246|140.85543|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ \ }} {{translated page|ja|宮城縣護國神社|version=101150390|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[simple:user:immanuelle/Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[sn:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮城縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyagi Gokoku Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Sendai]] [[Category:仙台市青葉区の建築物]] [[Category:仙台市青葉区の歴史]]
